did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture, specifically YOLOv8 (You Only Look Once version 8), to develop a web-based system for detecting seven types of skin cancer. The study involved training the model on a dataset of 3500 annotated skin images (later expanded via augmentation) and evaluated its performance using precision (0.975), recall (0.969), and mean Average Precision (mAP) for skin cancer detection.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a web-based skin cancer detection system using the YOLOv8 architecture. It details specific methods including data collection from Roboflow (3500 initial images, augmented to 8750), model training with specific hyperparameters (batch size 16, 50 epochs), and performance evaluation yielding new results (precision 0.975, recall 0.969). The structure includes standard research sections: Introduction, Theoretical Review, Methodology, Results and Discussion, and Conclusion, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Sistem Pendeteksi Penyakit Kanker Kulit Menggunakan Convolutional Neural Network Arsitektur YOLOv8 Berbasis Website
journal: Neptunus: Jurnal Ilmu Komputer Dan Teknologi Informasi
brief_summary: This study developed a web-based skin cancer detection system utilizing the YOLOv8 Convolutional Neural Network architecture to identify seven specific types of skin cancer. The model was trained on a dataset of 3,500 annotated images sourced from Roboflow, which was augmented to 8,750 samples and resized to 320x320 pixels. Performance evaluation employed precision, recall, and mean Average Precision (mAP) metrics alongside a confusion matrix to assess detection capabilities. The YOLOv8 model achieved a precision of 0.975, a recall of 0.969, and an overall accuracy of 89%. The system includes a web interface allowing users to upload images and receive real-time detection results. The authors conclude that the system provides accurate, rapid, and efficient results suitable for supporting early skin cancer diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Bounding box coordinates and class labels for seven types of skin cancer (nv, mel, bkl, bcc, akiec, vasc, df).
all_task_types.details: The paper uses the YOLOv8 architecture for object detection. The model predicts bounding boxes and class labels for skin cancer lesions. Evaluation metrics include precision, recall, and mean Average Precision (mAP), which are standard for detection tasks. The output includes confidence scores and bounding boxes for detected objects.

datasets_used.name: Roboflow Skin Cancer Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of annotated skin cancer images sourced from the Roboflow platform. The study initially collected 3,500 JPG images, which were annotated with bounding boxes and labels for seven classes using Roboflow. The dataset was subsequently augmented (using filters like rotation, grayscale, hue, saturation, brightness, exposure, and noise) to increase the sample size to 17,366 images. The dataset was split into training, validation, and testing sets.

use.training: Used to train the YOLOv8n and YOLOv5n models with specific hyperparameters (batch size 16/32, epochs 50, image size 320x320 or 640x640).
use.validation: Used to optimize the model during training and monitor performance.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the final model performance, including precision, recall, accuracy, and mAP, using a confusion matrix.
use.other: The initial subset of 3,500 images was used for annotation and initial splitting (75% train, 15% val, 10% test) before augmentation expanded the total count.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as precision, recall, accuracy, and mean Average Precision (mAP) derived from a confusion matrix.

YOLOv8n (Batch Size 16, Resolution 320x3.notes: Primary model configuration reported in abstract and conclusion. Evaluation used confusion matrix and mAP.

YOLOv8n (Batch Size 32, Resolution 640x6.notes: Skenario Pengujian Kedua. The paper describes the setup but does not explicitly report distinct metric values for this specific configuration in the text, grouping the final reported results (Precision 0.975, Recall 0.969, Accuracy 89%) under the general YOLOv8 performance.

YOLOv5n (Batch Size 16, Resolution 320x3.notes: Skenario Pengujian Ketiga. Comparison scenario described but specific metric values not explicitly detailed in the provided text chunks.

YOLOv5n (Batch Size 32, Resolution 640x6.notes: Skenario Pengujian Keempat. Comparison scenario described but specific metric values not explicitly detailed in the provided text chunks.

Roboflow Skin Cancer Dataset.notes: The paper reports overall performance for the YOLOv8 model as Precision: 0.975, Recall: 0.969, and Accuracy: 89%. It mentions evaluating mAP and using a confusion matrix. While four testing scenarios were defined (comparing YOLOv8n and YOLOv5n with different batch sizes and resolutions), the specific numerical results for the YOLOv5n models and the alternative YOLOv8n configuration are not explicitly listed as separate values in the text, with the primary reported metrics attributed to the YOLOv8 system.

architecture_used.main_model: YOLOv8n (You Only Look Once version 8 nano)

training_details.training_schedule: Training performed on Google Colab using data.yaml configuration; 50 epochs with batch sizes of 16 or 32 and image sizes of 320x320 or 640x640.

classification_or_output_head.output_format: Bounding box coordinates, class labels (7 classes: nv, mel, bkl, bcc, akiec, vasc, df), and confidence scores

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation using Roboflow filters: 90º Rotate, Rotation, Grayscale, Hue, Saturation, Brightness, Exposure, and Noise

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied via Roboflow filters: 90º Rotate and general Rotation.
Applied via Roboflow filters: Grayscale, Hue, Saturation, Brightness, and Exposure.
Initial dataset of 3,500 images was augmented to 8,750 images (as per Section 3.4) or 17,366 images (as per Section 4.2) using the listed Roboflow filters.

conclusions: The YOLOv8-based web system for skin cancer detection achieved high performance with a precision of 0.975, a recall of 0.969, and an overall accuracy of 89%.
The system successfully detected seven types of skin cancer (nv, mel, bkl, bcc, akiec, vasc, df) using a dataset of 3,500 initial images augmented to 17,366 samples.
The developed web interface allows users to upload images and receive real-time detection results, demonstrating the system's capability for rapid diagnosis.
The study concludes that the YOLOv8 model provides accurate and efficient results, making it a suitable tool for supporting early skin cancer diagnosis.
The integration of the YOLOv8 model into a web application demonstrates the feasibility of using deep learning for accessible, real-time dermatological screening.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single dataset sourced from Roboflow (derived from 3,500 initial images) without external validation on independent clinical datasets, limiting generalizability.
The dataset was heavily augmented (from 3,500 to 17,366 images) using Roboflow filters; the paper does not report per-class distribution or balance, raising potential concerns about class imbalance.

The paper reports conflicting dataset split ratios: Section 3.3 states a 75/15/10 split of the original 3,500 images, while Section 4.2 reports a 90/6/4 split of the augmented 17,366 images; the final testing set size is cited as 694 images.

While four testing scenarios were defined (comparing YOLOv8n and YOLOv5n with different batch sizes and resolutions), specific metric values for the YOLOv5n models and the alternative YOLOv8n configuration (batch 32, 640x640) are not explicitly reported in the text, with primary results attributed to YOLOv8n (batch 16, 320x640).
No statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals were reported; performance is evaluated solely using descriptive metrics (precision, recall, accuracy, mAP).
The system is a proof-of-concept web application; clinical relevance is limited as it has not been validated in a real-world clinical workflow or against dermatologist ground truth.

The paper does not discuss potential data leakage risks from the augmentation process or the source of the original Roboflow dataset (e.g., whether images are from diverse demographics or institutions).
